records.ety: [AS. elboga, elnboga (akin to D. elleboga, OHG. elinbogo, G. ellbogen, ellenbogen, Icel. <?/lnbogi; prop.; arm-bend); eln ell (orig., forearm) + boga a bending. See 1st Ell, and 4th Bow.]

defs: The joint or bend of the arm; the outer curve in the middle of the arm when bent.
quotes: Her arms to the elbows naked.

defs: Any turn or bend like that of the elbow, in a wall, building, and the like; a sudden turn in a line of coast or course of a river; also, an angular or jointed part of any structure, as the raised arm of a chair or sofa, or a short pipe fitting, turning at an angle or bent.
A sharp angle in any surface of wainscoting or other woodwork; the upright sides which flank any paneled work, as the sides of windows, where the jamb makes an elbow with the window back.
notes: ☞ Elbow is used adjectively or as part of a compound, to denote something shaped like, or acting like, an elbow; as, elbow joint; elbow tongs or elbow-tongs; elbowroom, elbow-room, or elbow room.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Elbowed (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Elbowing.]

defs: To push or hit with the elbow, as when one pushes by another.
quotes: They [the Dutch] would elbow our own aldermen off the Royal Exchange.

defs: To jut into an angle; to project or to bend after the manner of an elbow.
To push rudely along; to elbow one's way.
